one_line_takeaway: Garbled GCG suffixes secretly encode meaningful style/format/roleplay instructions; an off-the-shelf LLM can 'translate' them into fluent, low-perplexity natural-language prompts that transfer to black-box models, hitting 81.8% average ASR on HarmBench (7 closed LLMs) in <=10 queries.
key_finding: Translating garbled suffixes into readable prompts dramatically improves black-box transfer. On HarmBench the method reaches 81.8% average ASR across 7 closed-source LLMs within 10 queries, a +39.1% absolute gain over GCG-Advanced (42.6%) and +35.3% over the best baseline TAP (46.5%). On AdvBench it reaches 94.4% average, beating AdvPrompter (52.4%) and AutoDAN (42.1%), including >90% on the highly robust Llama-2-Chat models. Translated prompts are also stealthy: average perplexity ~14 versus ~2000 for garbled prompts, evading perplexity filters.
core_contribution: The paper proposes an 'adversarial prompt translation' method that converts garbled, gradient-optimized jailbreak suffixes (e.g., from GCG-Advanced) into coherent, human-readable natural-language adversarial prompts, and shows this makes the attacks far more transferable to black-box victim LLMs. The pipeline has two steps executed by an off-the-shelf 'translator' LLM (they select Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct): (1) an interpretation step where the translator is told the suffix flips the model from refusal to compliance and is asked to conjecture the semantic meaning hidden in the garbled suffix, and (2) a translation step that fuses those interpretations into N=10 natural-language prompts. The method is fully automatic: no manual prompt design, no hyper-parameter tuning, no additional model training, and no informative feedback from the victim model. Two advanced tricks are added on top: concatenating a second universal suffix collected from HarmBench, and 'random initialization by rephrasing' the harmful request before translation. A secondary contribution is a case study arguing the interpretations reveal NEW effective jailbreak-prompt design elements (e.g., 'detached/sarcastic tone'), which they inject into the Andriushchenko et al. adaptive-attack template.
threat_model: Deliberately strict, practical black-box transfer setting: the adversary CANNOT read the victim model's output token probabilities, CANNOT prefill/force the victim's output, and is limited to at most 10 queries per behavior (to avoid triggering rate-limits/bans on failed queries). Note that the ATTACK still requires white-box access to an open-source substitute/translator model to generate the initial gradient-based suffix; only the victim is black-box. This is a strictly stronger (more realistic) threat model than baselines like Adaptive Attack, AutoDAN, or PAL that need probability access or prefilling.
limitations: The paper has no dedicated limitations section; the following are grounded in its own results/setup. (1) The attack is not self-contained black-box: it depends on gradient-based suffixes from white-box open-source substitute models, so victims are only attacked via transfer. (2) Effectiveness is highly sensitive to which suffix is used - Table 6 shows worst-vs-best ASR spreads as large as 42.5% to 65.5% (Llama-3.1-8B suffixes) on a single victim. (3) The strongest safety-aligned victim, Claude-3-Sonnet, remains comparatively resistant at 57.5% (vs 88.0% on Claude-3-Haiku). (4) The translator LLM must itself be induced to comply with red-teaming (the templates instruct it to ignore ethical/legal constraints), so a better-aligned translator could refuse. (5) Only perplexity-based defense is empirically tested; robustness against semantic/paraphrase-aware or intent-classification defenses is not evaluated. This is an offensive red-teaming paper; it demonstrates a transferable, low-query, low-perplexity jailbreak but proposes no mitigation.

datasets_used: HarmBench - 200 standard harmful behaviors (ASR scored by HarmBench classifier)
AdvBench - 104 harmful behaviors (ASR scored by StrongREJECT GPT-4 judge, success = 1.0/1.0)
quantitative_results: Ours average ASR on HarmBench (7 closed-source LLMs): 81.8% (<=10 queries)
Per-victim Ours on HarmBench: GPT-3.5-Turbo 96.0%, GPT-4 70.0%, GPT-4-Turbo 88.0%, GPT-4o-mini 85.5%, GPT-4o 87.5%, Claude-3-Haiku 88.0%, Claude-3-Sonnet 57.5%
Absolute gains on HarmBench: +39.1% over GCG-Advanced, +35.3% over second-best TAP
Ours average ASR on AdvBench (6 victims): 94.4%; per-victim: GPT-3.5-Turbo-0301 100.0%, GPT-4 82.7%, Vicuna-7B-v1.5 100.0%, Llama-2-7B-Chat 93.3%, Llama-2-13B-Chat 90.4%, Mistral-7B-Instruct 100.0%
AdvBench absolute gains: +52.3% over AutoDAN, +42% over AdvPrompter
Perplexity (via Llama-2-7B): translated prompts ~14 vs garbled prompts ~2000
Ablation - interpretation step: +5.0% avg ASR (76.8% -> 81.8%); largest on Claude-3-Sonnet +9.5% (48.0% -> 57.5%)
Ablation - suffix concatenation: +6.5% avg (61.8% -> 68.3%); GPT-4 +12.0%, GPT-4-Turbo +22.0%
Ablation - pre-rephrasing (random init): +13.5% avg (68.3% -> 81.8%); GPT-4 46.5% -> 70.0%, GPT-4-Turbo 59.0% -> 88.0%
Translator-LLM choice on GPT-4o-mini: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct best at 47.1% avg; Vicuna-13B-translated prompts jump 29.5% -> 46.5% when translated by Llama-3.1-8B instead of Vicuna itself

Substitute-model suffix quality (Table 6, victim GPT-4o-mini, translator Llama-3.1-8B) avg/best/worst ASR: Llama-2-7B-Chat 32.4/36.5/28.0, Llama-2-13B-Chat 38.5/41.0/36.5, Vicuna-13B-v1.5 35.1/46.5/25.5, Mistral-7B-Instruct 41.0/44.5/36.0, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct 53.9/65.5/42.5
Case study - augmenting the AA template with newfound detached/sarcastic-tone rules raised ASR from 92.5% to 96.5% (GPT-3.5-Turbo) and 60.5% to 73.5% (GPT-4-Turbo)
baselines_compared: Direct Request (raw harmful request; 9.6% avg HarmBench, 0.0% AdvBench)
TAP (Mehrotra et al. 2023; 46.5% avg HarmBench, best baseline)
AA-Template / Adaptive Attack template (Andriushchenko et al. 2024; 34.6% avg HarmBench)
AutoDAN (Zhu et al. 2023; 42.1% avg AdvBench, numbers from AdvPrompter paper)
AdvPrompter (Paulus et al. 2024; 52.4% avg AdvBench, numbers from its own paper)
